title: Wildfire risk in the wildland–urban interface: A review of recent advances in measurement
abstract: This paper reviews advances in measuring wildfire risk in the wildland–urban interface (WUI), focusing on the multiple dimensions of risk including hazard, exposure, and vulnerability. We synthesize methods used to quantify each dimension and highlight key data needs and research gaps.
llm_reasoning: Directly addresses wildfire risk measurement in urban-adjacent areas, covering all key risk dimensions relevant to the study topic.

title: Structure ignitability in the wildland–urban interface: a review
abstract: We review the body of literature on structure ignitability in wildland–urban interface (WUI) environments, covering building materials, design features, and ember exposure. Findings are synthesized to provide guidance for reducing structural vulnerability to wildfire.
llm_reasoning: Focuses on structural vulnerability in the WUI, which is central to understanding urban exposure to wildfire.

title: Coupled fire–atmosphere simulation of the 2018 Camp Fire using WRF-Fire
abstract: We present a coupled fire–atmosphere simulation of the 2018 Camp Fire in Paradise, California, using the WRF-Fire model. Simulated fire spread, wind field evolution, and smoke transport are compared with observed fire perimeter data and satellite imagery.
llm_reasoning: Models fire spread and atmosphere dynamics for a major urban wildfire, providing insights into fire behavior relevant to the study topic.

title: Evacuee behavior and decision-making during the 2017 Thomas Fire
abstract: Using post-fire surveys of 500 households, we characterize evacuee decision-making, departure timing, and route selection during the 2017 Thomas Fire in Ventura County, California. We identify sociodemographic factors associated with delayed evacuation.
llm_reasoning: Studies evacuation behavior during a significant urban wildfire, providing empirical data on human response relevant to risk management.

title: Short-term respiratory health impacts of wildfire smoke in urban populations
abstract: We conduct a time-series analysis of emergency department visits for respiratory conditions in 12 California urban counties during wildfire smoke events from 2005–2018. Smoke exposure is quantified using a combination of PM2.5 monitoring data and satellite-derived smoke plume indicators.
llm_reasoning: Examines health impacts of wildfire smoke in urban areas, an important indirect wildfire risk pathway for urban populations.

title: Wildfire insurance losses and the role of building codes in the WUI
abstract: We analyze paid homeowner insurance claims from California wildfires between 2010 and 2020, examining the role of local building codes and construction standards in moderating losses. Properties in jurisdictions that adopted updated ignition-resistant construction codes experienced 34% lower expected losses.
llm_reasoning: Connects wildfire events to economic and insurance outcomes in the WUI, relevant to the financial risk dimension for urban systems.

title: Climate change projections of future wildfire risk in the western United States
abstract: Using an ensemble of CMIP6 climate models downscaled to 4-km resolution, we project future fire weather danger index, burned area, and WUI fire probability under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios for the western United States through 2100.
llm_reasoning: Projects future wildfire risk under climate change scenarios for a major wildfire-prone region, informing long-term urban risk planning.

title: Spatial analysis of wildfire risk exposure for California communities
abstract: We develop a spatially explicit index of wildfire risk exposure for all California census-designated places, integrating fire hazard severity zone classifications, historical fire frequency, and community-level vulnerability indicators.
llm_reasoning: Provides a comprehensive spatial risk assessment for California communities, directly addressing wildfire-linked risk in urban systems.

title: Land-use change and wildfire ignition risk in the wildland–urban interface
abstract: We model the relationship between land-use change trajectories and wildfire ignition risk in California's WUI from 1990 to 2020. Results show that residential expansion into fire-prone shrubland substantially increases ignition density.
llm_reasoning: Examines land-use change as a driver of wildfire risk in the WUI, relevant to urban growth and risk dynamics.

title: Policy barriers to wildfire-resilient communities: a qualitative analysis
abstract: Through 45 semi-structured interviews with local planners, fire officials, and policymakers across five western states, we identify institutional, financial, and regulatory barriers that impede the development of wildfire-resilient communities.
llm_reasoning: Analyzes governance and policy barriers to wildfire resilience, providing important context for understanding how risk is managed in urban systems.

title: Remote sensing of post-fire urban damage using high-resolution satellite imagery
abstract: We evaluate the ability of commercial high-resolution satellite imagery to detect and classify post-fire structural damage in the Paradise, California community following the 2018 Camp Fire. A convolutional neural network classifier achieves 91% overall accuracy in distinguishing destroyed from undamaged structures.
llm_reasoning: Applies remote sensing to assess post-fire urban damage, providing a methodological approach for monitoring wildfire impacts in urban systems.

title: Epidemiology of wildfire-related injuries and fatalities in the United States
abstract: We perform a systematic review and meta-analysis of peer-reviewed studies on wildfire-related injuries and fatalities in the United States, covering physical trauma, burns, smoke inhalation, and indirect mortality from stress and displacement.
llm_reasoning: Reviews health impacts of wildfires including mortality, relevant to understanding the human cost of wildfire risk in urban areas.

title: Community wildfire preparedness: a national survey of household readiness behaviors
abstract: We report results of a nationally representative survey of 3,000 U.S. households in the WUI, measuring awareness of local fire risk, household preparedness actions taken, and perceived barriers to preparedness.
llm_reasoning: Surveys household preparedness in the WUI, relevant to the social dimensions of urban wildfire risk management.

title: The role of defensible space in reducing structure loss in WUI wildfires
abstract: Using parcel-level data from five California wildfires, we test whether compliance with vegetation management (defensible space) standards reduces the probability of structure loss. Properties with compliant defensible space were 40% less likely to burn.
llm_reasoning: Directly examines the effectiveness of defensible space in reducing structure loss in WUI wildfires, highly relevant to urban exposure and vulnerability.

title: Fire weather and ignition patterns across the California WUI, 1992–2020
abstract: We analyze spatial and temporal patterns of fire ignition in California's WUI from 1992 to 2020, relating ignition density to fire weather indices, land-cover types, and proximity to roads and power lines.
llm_reasoning: Examines fire ignition patterns in the WUI over a long time series, relevant to understanding spatiotemporal wildfire risk dynamics.

title: Economic vulnerability of wildfire-exposed households: evidence from mortgage default data
abstract: We link parcel-level wildfire exposure to mortgage default records in California to test whether wildfire events trigger financial distress for homeowners. We find a significant increase in mortgage default rates within 2 km of fire perimeters in the three years following a wildfire.
llm_reasoning: Links wildfire exposure to financial outcomes for households, extending the economic risk dimension of wildfires beyond direct property loss.

title: Sentinel-2 multi-temporal analysis of WUI burn severity and vegetation recovery
abstract: Using Sentinel-2 multispectral imagery, we map burn severity and track post-fire vegetation recovery trajectories in WUI areas of southern California over a five-year period. Results show that high burn severity significantly delays native shrubland recovery.
llm_reasoning: Analyzes burn severity and vegetation recovery in WUI areas, relevant to understanding landscape dynamics following wildfire.

title: Wildfire-driven displacement and housing insecurity in California
abstract: Drawing on Red Cross shelter registration data and longitudinal survey follow-up of 800 displaced households, we examine the duration and nature of post-wildfire displacement and identify factors associated with long-term housing insecurity.
llm_reasoning: Examines post-wildfire displacement and housing insecurity, relevant to understanding longer-term social impacts of wildfire on urban populations.

title: Integrating social vulnerability into wildfire risk assessment frameworks
abstract: We propose an integrated wildfire risk assessment framework that incorporates social vulnerability dimensions—including poverty, age, disability, and language isolation—alongside biophysical hazard and exposure metrics. The framework is applied to Arizona's WUI communities.
llm_reasoning: Integrates social vulnerability into wildfire risk assessment, directly addressing equity dimensions of wildfire risk in urban systems.

title: Wildfire smoke transport and urban air quality: a high-resolution modeling study
abstract: We use the WRF-Chem model at 1-km horizontal resolution to simulate wildfire smoke transport and its impact on fine particulate matter (PM2.5) concentrations in six major western U.S. cities during the summer of 2020. Model output is validated against EPA air quality monitoring network data.
llm_reasoning: Models wildfire smoke transport and urban air quality impacts, providing high-resolution insights into this important health risk pathway.
